stem: [RW] A practical nurse is teaching an adult client newly fitted with a rigid leg brace how to prevent skin breakdown. Which client statement indicates the need for further teaching?
answerChoices: I will remove the brace at scheduled times to inspect my skin for red areas or rubbing.
I will tighten the brace straps as much as possible so the brace does not shift while I walk.
I will wear a clean, wrinkle-free sock under the brace unless told otherwise.
I will report numbness, tingling, or persistent pressure points to the nurse right away.
correctAnswerText: I will tighten the brace straps as much as possible so the brace does not shift while I walk.
rationale: Straps should be secure but not maximally tight. Over-tightening increases focal pressure, impairs circulation, and raises risk of skin injury. Routine skin checks, protective sock use, and early reporting of neurovascular warning signs are appropriate safety behaviors.
whyWrong: Scheduled brace removal with skin inspection is a correct prevention strategy because early redness or rubbing can be addressed before breakdown occurs.
A thin, clean, wrinkle-free sock helps reduce friction and shear under a rigid brace and supports skin protection.
Numbness, tingling, or persistent pressure can indicate neurovascular compromise or poor brace fit and should be reported promptly.
publicPilotNotice: [RW] New rewrite pending review. Not approved for publication or student-facing use.
revisionPlan: Stage 3 critical rewrite: upgraded distractor plausibility and instruction framing; removed easy elimination cues; retained negative-stem safety-teaching target. Awaiting review.

stem: An older adult with severe vision loss has eaten very little breakfast and most food remains untouched on one side of the plate. Which action should the practical nurse take first?
answerChoices: Orient the tray using clock positions and ask whether the client can locate each food item.
Remove the untouched food and document that the client refused part of the meal.
Ask dietary services to send only finger foods for future meals.
Tell the client to feel across the plate with a hand to find the remaining food.
correctAnswerText: Orient the tray using clock positions and ask whether the client can locate each food item.
rationale: Food left on one side of the plate may mean the client cannot locate the food, not that the client is refusing to eat. The first nursing action is to make the tray easier to understand by using clock positions and checking whether the client can find the food. This supports safety, nutrition, and independence.
whyWrong: Documenting refusal is premature because the nurse has not checked whether severe vision loss kept the client from finding food on the tray.
Changing future meals to finger foods skips the immediate assessment and may reduce independence before trying tray orientation.
Having the client sweep the plate by hand is less organized and can increase spills compared with predictable clock-position cues.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka after review. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Emeka reviewed and approved. Metadata fix confirmed. Moved to alpha pool.

stem: [RW] An older adult with severe vision loss is learning to use a long-handled shoehorn for independent dressing. Which client action shows correct technique?
answerChoices: The client rocks side to side while pushing the foot down hard so the shoe goes on faster.
The client keeps the shoehorn at the heel and slides the foot gently into the shoe without forcing.
The client stands on one leg and twists the foot repeatedly until the shoe slides in.
The client places the shoehorn over the top of the foot and presses downward to seat the shoe.
correctAnswerText: The client keeps the shoehorn at the heel and slides the foot gently into the shoe without forcing.
rationale: Correct shoehorn use places the blade at the heel and allows the foot to glide in with controlled, gentle motion. Forcing, twisting, or unstable positioning increases risk of skin injury and loss of balance.
whyWrong: Forceful downward pressure and rocking reduce stability and can cause loss of balance or skin shear at the heel.
Standing on one leg while repeatedly twisting increases fall risk and does not reflect safe assistive-device technique.
Pressing from the top of the foot mispositions the shoehorn and can create pressure injury instead of guiding the heel safely.
publicPilotNotice: [RW] New rewrite replacing quarantined item. Not approved for publication or student-facing use.
revisionPlan: Stage 3 revise-once: replaced out-of-place distractor with clinically plausible unsafe techniques and increased requirement for true safe-use judgment. Awaiting review.

stem: An older adult with severe vision loss has food untouched on one side of the plate. Which response by the practical nurse best supports safe independent eating?
answerChoices: Orient the meal tray by describing each item according to its hour position.
Tell the client to feel across the plate to identify foods.
Move items around during the meal without explaining the change.
Ask family to feed the client because vision is limited.
correctAnswerText: Orient the meal tray by describing each item according to its hour position.
rationale: Hour-position meal orientation promotes independence and reduces spills because the client can locate each food item predictably. The other options delay assessment, increase risk, or reduce safe independent use.
whyWrong: Feeling across the plate may help locate food, but it is less safe and less dignified than giving a predictable clock-position map first.
Moving items without explaining the change makes the tray harder to trust and can confuse a client who depends on consistent placement.
Asking family to feed the client reduces independence and is not the first nursing response when tray orientation can support self-feeding.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka + Ihechi quorum after rationale cleanup. Keep internal until final human approval.

stem: [RW] An adult client is being discharged after treatment for acute vertigo. Which client statement shows correct understanding of home safety instructions during a dizziness episode?
answerChoices: I will walk slowly without help to train my balance when the spinning starts.
I will bend quickly to pick up objects so I can finish tasks before symptoms worsen.
I will sit or lie down immediately when the room starts spinning and wait until symptoms settle before standing.
I will drive myself a short distance if I need fresh air because slow driving is safer.
correctAnswerText: I will sit or lie down immediately when the room starts spinning and wait until symptoms settle before standing.
rationale: During active vertigo, the priority is fall prevention. Sitting or lying down immediately reduces injury risk until symptoms improve. Unassisted ambulation, rapid position changes, and driving during dizziness increase risk of falls or accidents.
whyWrong: Walking unassisted during active vertigo increases fall risk; activity should resume only after symptoms improve and safety is reassessed.
Rapid bending can worsen vertigo and destabilize balance, increasing the chance of a fall.
Driving during dizziness is unsafe because vertigo can impair spatial orientation and reaction time.
publicPilotNotice: [RW] New rewrite pending review. Not approved for publication or student-facing use.
revisionPlan: Stage 3 critical rewrite: converted all distractors to realistic but unsafe client instruction statements; removed framing mismatch and strengthened discharge-teaching validity. Awaiting review.

stem: An adult with spinning dizziness reaches for the wall when standing. Which response by the practical nurse is safest?
answerChoices: Have the client sit or lie down and call for help before walking.
Leave the client standing while looking for discharge papers.
Offer fluids and then allow unassisted ambulation.
Dim the lights and ask the client to walk slowly to the lobby.
correctAnswerText: Have the client sit or lie down and call for help before walking.
rationale: Vertigo increases fall risk; limiting ambulation and getting assistance are the safest immediate measures. The other options delay assessment, increase risk, or reduce safe independent use.
whyWrong: Leaving the client standing during spinning dizziness keeps the fall risk active when the safest first step is to get the client seated or lying down.
Fluids may be appropriate later for some clients, but they do not solve the immediate risk of falling while dizzy.
Dim lights may reduce stimulation, but asking a dizzy client to walk slowly still delays the priority safety action.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka after why-wrong verification. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Emeka confirmed why-wrong explanations are clinical-specific. Concept passed. Moved to alpha pool.

stem: A practical nurse is caring for adult reporting spinning dizziness in the urgent care discharge area. Which action has the highest priority?
answerChoices: Walk beside the client slowly to the lobby and reassess dizziness there.
Finish the discharge teaching first because the client is already leaving.
Have the client sit or lie down and call for help before walking.
Offer fluids while the client remains standing at the counter.
correctAnswerText: Have the client sit or lie down and call for help before walking.
rationale: Vertigo increases fall risk; limiting ambulation and getting assistance are the safest immediate measures. The other options delay assessment, increase risk, or reduce safe independent use.
whyWrong: Walking the client to the lobby while dizziness is active still exposes the client to a fall before the nurse stabilizes the situation.
Finishing discharge teaching first delays the immediate safety action; fall prevention comes before routine discharge tasks.
Offering fluids while the client remains standing does not remove the immediate fall risk from sudden spinning dizziness.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka + Ihechi quorum after rationale cleanup. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Approved alpha item; option order rotated so the correct answer is not always A while keeping the same clinical key.

stem: A client learning to use a lower-limb prosthesis says, "I am worried I will not manage this safely at home." Which response by the practical nurse is most therapeutic?
answerChoices: Let's review the steps together and identify when you should call for help.
You will feel more confident once you have practiced with it for a few days.
You need to be more independent with this equipment.
Wear the prosthesis longer when soreness develops to toughen the skin.
correctAnswerText: Let's review the steps together and identify when you should call for help.
rationale: The best response accepts the client's concern and turns it into a practical safety plan. Reviewing the steps together helps the client practice, ask questions, and know when to call for help. This supports confidence without dismissing the client's fear.
whyWrong: Simple reassurance may sound supportive, but it does not assess the client’s specific safety concern or build a plan for when to call for help.
Pushing independence before checking the client’s skills and concerns can increase injury risk and shuts down therapeutic communication.
Soreness can signal pressure or poor fit; wearing the prosthesis longer can worsen skin injury instead of protecting the residual limb.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka after distractor review. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Emeka confirmed distractors are plausible but wrong. Cartoonish distractor replaced. Moved to alpha pool.

stem: The nurse is reviewing prosthesis and residual-limb care with a client. Which client statement requires immediate correction?
answerChoices: I should keep wearing it if a red area is painful but not bleeding.
I will check my skin every time I remove the prosthesis.
I will report redness that does not fade after I remove the prosthesis.
I will keep the residual limb clean and dry before applying the prosthesis.
correctAnswerText: I should keep wearing it if a red area is painful but not bleeding.
rationale: A painful red area can be an early sign of pressure injury even if the skin is not bleeding. The client should remove the prosthesis and report persistent redness or pain instead of continuing to wear it. The other statements show safer prosthesis and residual-limb care.
whyWrong: Checking the skin each time the prosthesis is removed helps catch pressure injury early, so this statement shows safe understanding.
Reporting redness that does not fade is appropriate because persistent redness can be an early warning sign of tissue damage.
Keeping the residual limb clean and dry reduces irritation and infection risk and helps the prosthesis fit more safely.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka after stem clarity review. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Emeka confirmed stem clarity is improved. Moved to alpha pool.

stem: A client is learning home care for a lower-limb prosthesis and residual limb. Which instruction should the practical nurse reinforce as the priority for preventing skin injury?
answerChoices: Massage an open area before reapplying the prosthesis.
Inspect the residual limb after removing the prosthesis and report persistent redness or skin breakdown.
Skip sock changes when the socket feels loose.
Apply a heating pad to the residual limb before each fitting.
correctAnswerText: Inspect the residual limb after removing the prosthesis and report persistent redness or skin breakdown.
rationale: Skin inspection detects pressure injury early; persistent redness or breakdown can worsen if the prosthesis is reapplied without follow-up. The other options delay assessment, increase risk, or reduce safe independent use.
whyWrong: Massaging an open area can worsen tissue injury and does not address the need to stop using the prosthesis and report breakdown.
Skipping sock changes can make the socket fit poorly, increasing friction, pressure, and skin breakdown risk.
Heat can burn or irritate vulnerable tissue and should not be used as a routine step before fitting the prosthesis.
publicPilotNotice: Candidate alpha item approved by Emeka + Ihechi quorum after rationale cleanup. Keep internal until final human approval.
revisionPlan: Approved alpha item; option order rotated so the correct answer is not always A while keeping the same clinical key.

stem: A nurse is setting up lunch for a client with severe macular degeneration. Which instruction best helps the client locate each food item independently?
answerChoices: Explain the tray using clock positions.
Review the recommended food groups on the plate.
Place all foods in separate bowls.
Tell the client to feel each item before eating.
correctAnswerText: Explain the tray using clock positions.
rationale: Clock positions give the client a simple, repeatable way to locate foods without seeing the plate. Food-group teaching and bowl placement do not provide precise spatial orientation, and touching each food is unnecessary and less dignified.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: During assessment, a client says, "The room spins sometimes, but other days I just feel lightheaded." Which statement by the nurse is most accurate?
answerChoices: Vertigo occurs only as a sudden short-term problem.
Vertigo can occur as either a short-term or persistent condition.
Lightheadedness and vertigo always mean the same thing.
Dizziness rules out a vestibular cause.
correctAnswerText: Vertigo can occur as either a short-term or persistent condition.
rationale: Vertigo may be acute, recurrent, or chronic. Dizziness is a broader symptom and may or may not include vertigo, so absolute statements are unsafe.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: At a home visit, a client with diabetes and a transtibial prosthesis says the device feels so good that it stays on all day and all night except during bathing. What should the nurse emphasize first?
answerChoices: How to polish and store the prosthetic socket
Daily removal of the prosthesis to assess the residual limb
Praise for tolerating the prosthesis for long periods
Ways to increase walking distance each week
correctAnswerText: Daily removal of the prosthesis to assess the residual limb
rationale: The priority is protecting skin and tissue on the residual limb. A client with diabetes needs routine inspection because pressure and irritation may progress before discomfort is noticed.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A client with a below-knee prosthesis reports that the residual limb becomes puffy in the evening after the device is removed. Which question is most useful?
answerChoices: Do you leave the limb uncovered when the prosthesis is off?
Has the prosthesis always felt too tight during walking?
Does swelling disappear if you loosen the shoe?
Are you using the ordered shrinker or elastic wrap after removal?
correctAnswerText: Are you using the ordered shrinker or elastic wrap after removal?
rationale: Compression when the prosthesis is not worn helps control edema and maintain limb shape. Swelling should not be dismissed or attributed to prosthesis fit without assessing compression use.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: Two weeks after a traumatic forearm amputation, a client says, "My missing hand burns and aches." Which explanation best fits this report?
answerChoices: Psychogenic pain caused by poor coping
Neurologic phantom limb pain
Pain from delayed wound closure
Infection in the removed hand
correctAnswerText: Neurologic phantom limb pain
rationale: Pain felt in an absent limb is phantom limb pain and is linked to nervous system activity after amputation. It should not be dismissed as psychogenic or attributed to tissue that is no longer present.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A client with a healed above-knee amputation reports intermittent aching where the lower leg used to be. Which nurse teaching is appropriate as an initial comfort strategy?
answerChoices: Stop wearing the prosthesis until all sensations disappear.
Use gentle massage or touch techniques on the residual limb.
Ask for psychotherapy before using physical comfort measures.
Keep the residual limb completely untouched.
correctAnswerText: Use gentle massage or touch techniques on the residual limb.
rationale: Gentle massage or desensitization-style touch can be used as a nonpharmacologic approach for phantom pain. Avoiding all touch or stopping prosthesis use is not routinely indicated.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A client is discharged with a lower-leg orthotic brace after a sports injury. Which instruction best reduces skin breakdown under the brace?
answerChoices: Apply the liner flat and evenly, with a snug but not constricting fit.
Pull the liner very tight so the limb cannot swell.
Leave the liner loose so air can circulate.
Add extra padding only over the middle of the calf.
correctAnswerText: Apply the liner flat and evenly, with a snug but not constricting fit.
rationale: A smooth, evenly applied liner reduces friction and pressure. Padding that is too tight, too loose, or uneven can create localized injury.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A nurse reviews home care for a client who wears an ankle-foot orthosis during the day. Which pair of instructions should be included?
answerChoices: Check the skin each day and wash the limb with mild soap and water before reapplying the brace.
Reapply the brace over bare skin at bedtime and tighten straps to prevent movement.
Skip daily skin checks and remove the brace only when discomfort becomes severe.
Apply lotion under the brace before each use and keep the sock slightly damp for comfort.
correctAnswerText: Check the skin each day and wash the limb with mild soap and water before reapplying the brace.
rationale: Routine inspection and gentle cleansing help prevent and detect irritation. The brace should not be worn over unprotected skin during sleep unless specifically ordered and properly padded.
whyWrong: Overnight bare-skin wear with tight straps increases pressure and friction injury risk.
Waiting for severe discomfort delays detection of early skin breakdown and pressure injury.
Moisture and lotion under an orthosis increase shear and skin maceration risk.

stem: A client with an essential tremor spills food from a standard fork despite adequate grip strength. Which adaptive device is most appropriate to try first?
answerChoices: Weighted eating utensils
Only a soup spoon for all foods
Child-sized utensils
Extra-long utensils
correctAnswerText: Weighted eating utensils
rationale: Weighted utensils can improve control for some clients with tremor and support independent feeding. Changing size or shape alone does not address tremor control as directly.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: An older adult asks how to clean removable dentures before bedtime. Which instruction should the nurse give?
answerChoices: Scrub firmly with a stiff brush to remove all stains.
Soak them in household bleach after each meal.
Rinse with boiling water to disinfect them.
Brush gently with a soft brush and place them in denture solution overnight.
correctAnswerText: Brush gently with a soft brush and place them in denture solution overnight.
rationale: Dentures should be cleaned gently and soaked in an appropriate solution. Stiff brushing, bleach, and very hot water can damage the appliance.
whyWrong: Aggressive scrubbing can scratch denture surfaces, which traps debris and bacteria.
Household bleach is too harsh for oral appliances and can damage the denture and irritate oral tissue.
Boiling water can warp dental materials and increase oral injury risk.

stem: A client with moderate hearing loss declines hearing aids but wants to continue telephone conversations. Which feature set would be most helpful?
answerChoices: Caption display, amplified ring, visual call signal, and voice capability
A smaller handset with no screen
A phone that disables voice communication
A standard phone placed in a quiet room only
correctAnswerText: Caption display, amplified ring, visual call signal, and voice capability
rationale: A captioned/amplified phone can support both seeing and hearing communication cues. A standard phone or a device without voice features may not meet the client's needs.
whyWrong: No screen removes visual caption support, which is a key accommodation for hearing loss.
Disabling voice communication removes an essential communication channel rather than supporting it.
A standard phone alone lacks amplification, captioning, and visual alerts needed for moderate hearing loss.

stem: A client has left-sided leg weakness after hip surgery. The right leg is stronger. Which cane teaching is correct?
answerChoices: Hold the cane in the left hand with the elbow locked.
Hold the cane in the right hand with slight elbow flexion.
Hold the cane in the left hand with the arm fully extended.
Switch hands every few steps to avoid fatigue.
correctAnswerText: Hold the cane in the right hand with slight elbow flexion.
rationale: The cane is typically held on the stronger side, with the elbow slightly bent, to provide balance and support for the weaker side.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A client with a non-weight-bearing ankle injury says, "The crutches can take the place of my bad leg, so I can lean on them however I need." Which response is best?
answerChoices: Crutches should be used exactly as taught to reduce injury risk.
One crutch can safely replace the injured leg at all times.
Crutches are needed only for walking outdoors.
Use the crutches only when pain becomes severe.
correctAnswerText: Crutches should be used exactly as taught to reduce injury risk.
rationale: Crutches are assistive devices that require correct technique and consistent safety use. Treating them as a limb replacement or using them inconsistently can increase fall or nerve-injury risk.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: While observing a client using axillary crutches, which teaching point is most important?
answerChoices: Support body weight through the hand grips.
Press the tops of the crutches firmly into the armpits.
Keep both elbows completely straight while walking.
Remove worn rubber tips so the crutches slide smoothly.
correctAnswerText: Support body weight through the hand grips.
rationale: Weight should be supported through the hands on the grips. Axillary pressure may injure nerves and vessels, locked elbows reduce control, and missing tips increase fall risk.
whyWrong: Forceful technique increases injury risk and defeats the safety purpose of the assistive device.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A client reports ear pain after pushing a cotton swab into the ear after swimming. Which teaching should the nurse provide?
answerChoices: Use cotton swabs in the canal only when the ear is wet.
Insert the swab only if the cotton tip is dry.
Rotate the swab gently inside the canal.
Clean only the outer ear with cotton swabs.
correctAnswerText: Clean only the outer ear with cotton swabs.
rationale: Cotton swabs should be used only on the external ear. Inserting them into the canal can injure tissue or push material deeper.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A landscaper feels sharp eye pain after debris blows under protective glasses. What instruction is safest?
answerChoices: Seek urgent eye evaluation for assessment and possible irrigation.
Schedule a routine visit later in the week.
Patch the eye at home and use warm compresses for several days.
Wait to see whether blinking removes the object overnight.
correctAnswerText: Seek urgent eye evaluation for assessment and possible irrigation.
rationale: A suspected foreign body with eye pain requires prompt evaluation to reduce the risk of corneal abrasion, retained debris, or infection.
whyWrong: It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.
Forceful technique increases injury risk and defeats the safety purpose of the assistive device.
It is lower-priority or unsafe compared with the best nursing action in this scenario.

stem: A nurse is caring for a client with Clostridioides difficile infection. Which action by the nurse requires immediate correction?
answerChoices: The nurse places the client in a private room with contact precautions signage.
The nurse washes hands with soap and water after removing gloves in the client room.
The nurse uses an alcohol-based hand rub instead of soap and water after direct contact with soiled linens.
The nurse dons a gown and gloves before entering the client room.
correctAnswerText: The nurse uses an alcohol-based hand rub instead of soap and water after direct contact with soiled linens.
rationale: C difficile spores are not eliminated by alcohol-based hand rubs. Soap and water with mechanical friction is required to physically remove spores from hands after contact with C difficile clients or their environment.
whyWrong: Handwashing with soap and water is the correct method for C difficile and does not require correction.
Alcohol-based rubs do not kill C difficile spores; soap and water is required.
Contact precautions with gown and gloves are appropriate for C difficile.

stem: A practical nurse is teaching a client how to perform hand hygiene before preparing food at home. Which statement by the client indicates correct understanding?
answerChoices: I will rub my hands together with soap for at least 10 seconds under running water.
I will use hand sanitizer only since it is faster than washing.
I will scrub all surfaces of my hands with soap and running water for at least 20 seconds.
I will rinse my hands first, then apply soap and rinse again quickly.
correctAnswerText: I will scrub all surfaces of my hands with soap and running water for at least 20 seconds.
rationale: The recommended handwashing technique requires scrubbing all hand surfaces with soap under running water for at least 20 seconds to effectively remove transient microorganisms before food preparation.
whyWrong: Ten seconds is insufficient; 20 seconds is the minimum recommended duration.
Hand sanitizer is not a substitute for handwashing before food preparation when hands are visibly soiled.
Rinsing first then briefly applying soap does not allow adequate contact time for microbial removal.

stem: A nurse is preparing to insert a urinary catheter. Which action best demonstrates sterile technique?
answerChoices: The nurse opens the sterile catheter kit on the client bedside table.
The nurse drops the catheter into the sterile tray without touching the tray edges.
The nurse uses clean gloves to hold the catheter during insertion.
The nurse places the sterile drape on the bed before washing hands.
correctAnswerText: The nurse drops the catheter into the sterile tray without touching the tray edges.
rationale: Dropping the catheter into the sterile field without touching the edges maintains sterility. Touching the tray edges, using clean instead of sterile gloves, or placing the drape before hand hygiene all break sterile technique.
whyWrong: Opening a kit on a non-sterile surface contaminates the sterile field.
Clean gloves introduce microorganisms; sterile gloves are required for catheter insertion.
Hand hygiene must be performed before handling any sterile equipment or drapes.

stem: A nurse is teaching a group of older adults about fall prevention at home. Which statement by a participant indicates a need for further teaching?
answerChoices: I will keep a nightlight on in my bedroom and bathroom.
I will remove loose rugs from hallways and near stairs.
I will wear soft-soled slippers without backs when walking around the house.
I will install grab bars near the toilet and inside the shower.
correctAnswerText: I will wear soft-soled slippers without backs when walking around the house.
rationale: Slippers without backs do not secure the heel and can slip off or cause tripping. Supportive footwear with non-slip soles and a secure fit is recommended for fall prevention in older adults.
whyWrong: Nightlights improve visibility and reduce fall risk during nighttime bathroom trips.
Removing loose rugs eliminates a common tripping hazard.
Grab bars near toilets and showers provide stability support and reduce fall risk in wet areas.

stem: A nurse is performing a wellness check on a 65-year-old client. Which screening is most appropriate to recommend at this visit?
answerChoices: Bone density scan for osteoporosis screening
Colonoscopy for colorectal cancer screening
Mammogram for breast cancer screening
All of the above are appropriate for this age group
correctAnswerText: All of the above are appropriate for this age group
rationale: At age 65, guidelines support bone density screening, colorectal cancer screening, and mammography. All three are recommended preventive screenings for this age group.
whyWrong: Bone density screening is recommended but is only one of several appropriate screenings at this age.
Colonoscopy is recommended starting at age 45-50 but is not the only appropriate screening.
Mammography is recommended but does not replace other age-appropriate screenings.

stem: A practical nurse is teaching a new mother about infant car seat safety. Which instruction is most important?
answerChoices: Place the infant in a rear-facing car seat in the back seat of the vehicle.
Use a forward-facing car seat once the infant can sit up unsupported.
Place the car seat in the front passenger seat for easier monitoring.
Transition to a booster seat when the infant weighs 15 pounds.
correctAnswerText: Place the infant in a rear-facing car seat in the back seat of the vehicle.
rationale: Infants must ride rear-facing in the back seat until they reach the maximum height or weight limit of their car seat. This position protects the head, neck, and spine in a collision.
whyWrong: Forward-facing is not recommended until at least age 2 or when the child exceeds the rear-facing seat limits.
The front passenger seat is dangerous due to airbag deployment risk.
A booster seat is for older children; infants need a rear-facing car seat, not a booster.

stem: A client tells the nurse, 'I feel like nobody cares about me anymore since my husband died.' Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?
answerChoices: You should not feel that way; your family loves you very much.
Tell me more about what it has been like for you since your husband passed.
Have you thought about joining a support group for bereavement?
I understand exactly how you feel because I lost someone too.
correctAnswerText: Tell me more about what it has been like for you since your husband passed.
rationale: This open-ended response encourages the client to express feelings and validates their experience. It uses therapeutic communication by exploring the client's perspective without minimizing or redirecting.
whyWrong: This response dismisses the client's feelings and contradicts their lived experience.
While potentially helpful, this deflects from the immediate emotional expression rather than exploring it first.
This shifts focus to the nurse's experience and is not client-centered.

stem: A nurse is caring for a client with major depressive disorder who has not eaten for two days. Which action is the nurse's priority?
answerChoices: Encourage the client to join the group dining room for socialization.
Assess the client's nutritional status and report to the healthcare provider.
Offer the client a choice of favorite foods to stimulate appetite.
Document the client's refusal to eat in the medical record.
correctAnswerText: Assess the client's nutritional status and report to the healthcare provider.
rationale: With two days of no intake, the priority is a focused nutritional assessment and reporting to the provider for potential intervention (e.g., nutritional supplements, medication adjustment, or meal planning).
whyWrong: Socialization may help but does not address the immediate nutritional deficit or need for provider notification.
Offering favorites may be tried but is not the priority when two days of no intake requires clinical assessment and intervention.
Documenting refusal without assessment and reporting is negligent; clinical action is needed first.

stem: A client with schizophrenia tells the nurse that voices are telling them to hurt themselves. What is the nurse's best initial response?
answerChoices: Tell the client that the voices are not real and should be ignored.
Ask the client what the voices are saying and assess for suicidal intent.
Redirect the client to a group activity to distract from the voices.
Administer PRN antipsychotic medication immediately without further assessment.
correctAnswerText: Ask the client what the voices are saying and assess for suicidal intent.
rationale: When a client reports command hallucinations with self-harm content, the nurse must assess the specific content and the client's intent to follow the commands. This assessment determines the level of intervention needed.
whyWrong: Dismissing the voices invalidates the client's experience and may increase distress.
Distraction does not address the safety risk posed by command hallucinations.
Medication may be needed but assessment of the specific risk must come first to guide treatment.

stem: A nurse is caring for a client with heart failure who has gained 3 pounds overnight. Which action should the nurse take first?
answerChoices: Restrict the client's fluid intake to 1000 mL per day.
Notify the healthcare provider of the weight change.
Auscultate the client's lung sounds for crackles.
Encourage the client to ambulate to promote fluid mobilization.
correctAnswerText: Auscultate the client's lung sounds for crackles.
rationale: A rapid weight gain in a heart failure client suggests fluid retention. The nurse's first action is to assess for pulmonary congestion by auscultating lung sounds before notifying the provider or implementing interventions.
whyWrong: Fluid restriction may be needed but assessment of the clinical impact must come first.
Notification to the provider is important but the nurse should gather assessment data first.
Ambulation may worsen symptoms if the client is in respiratory distress from fluid overload.

stem: A client with type 2 diabetes has a blood glucose of 52 mg/dL and is diaphoretic and trembling. Which action should the nurse take first?
answerChoices: Administer 10 units of regular insulin subcutaneously.
Give the client 4 ounces of orange juice or glucose tablets.
Recheck the blood glucose in 15 minutes.
Notify the healthcare provider immediately.
correctAnswerText: Give the client 4 ounces of orange juice or glucose tablets.
rationale: Blood glucose of 52 mg/dL with symptoms of hypoglycemia requires immediate treatment with fast-acting glucose (15-20 grams of simple carbohydrates). This is the priority before rechecking or notifying the provider.
whyWrong: Insulin would further lower blood glucose and is contraindicated in hypoglycemia.
Rechecking can wait until after the client receives glucose treatment; the current value already confirms hypoglycemia.
Provider notification is needed but immediate glucose administration is the life-saving priority.

stem: A nurse is assessing a client 4 hours after a total knee replacement. The client reports severe pain rated 9/10 and the dressing is saturated with blood. What is the nurse's priority action?
answerChoices: Administer the prescribed PRN pain medication immediately.
Elevate the leg and apply ice while notifying the healthcare provider.
Document the findings and continue to monitor every 15 minutes.
Remove the dressing to inspect the surgical site.
correctAnswerText: Elevate the leg and apply ice while notifying the healthcare provider.
rationale: Severe pain with saturated dressing after knee replacement suggests possible hemorrhage. The priority is to elevate and apply ice (to reduce bleeding and swelling) while notifying the provider for possible surgical intervention.
whyWrong: Pain medication does not address the potential hemorrhage and may mask worsening symptoms.
Continuing to monitor without intervention delays treatment for a potential post-surgical complication.
Removing the dressing could disrupt any clot formation and worsen bleeding.

stem: A practical nurse is administering digoxin to a client with heart failure. The client's apical pulse is 58 beats per minute. What should the nurse do?
answerChoices: Administer the digoxin as prescribed since the heart failure requires it.
Hold the digoxin and notify the healthcare provider of the pulse rate.
Give half the dose and recheck the pulse in 30 minutes.
Administer the digoxin with food to slow absorption.
correctAnswerText: Hold the digoxin and notify the healthcare provider of the pulse rate.
rationale: Digoxin is held when the apical pulse is below 60 bpm in adults because the drug slows heart rate further and can cause dangerous bradycardia or heart block. The nurse must notify the provider before administering.
whyWrong: Administering digoxin with a pulse below 60 bpm risks severe bradycardia or cardiac arrest.
Half-dosing is not an acceptable response to a low pulse; the dose should be withheld entirely.
Food does not affect the cardiac risk associated with administering digoxin to a bradycardic client.

stem: A nurse is teaching a client who is prescribed warfarin about dietary considerations. Which statement by the client indicates correct understanding?
answerChoices: I will eat a consistent amount of leafy green vegetables each week.
I will increase my intake of spinach and kale to improve my nutrition.
I will avoid all vegetables while taking this medication.
I will take a vitamin K supplement daily to prevent bleeding.
correctAnswerText: I will eat a consistent amount of leafy green vegetables each week.
rationale: Vitamin K-rich foods (leafy greens) affect warfarin's anticoagulant effect. The key is consistency, not avoidance. Sudden increases or decreases in vitamin K intake can cause dangerous INR fluctuations.
whyWrong: Increasing vitamin K intake counteracts warfarin and can lead to clotting.
Complete avoidance of vegetables is unnecessary and nutritionally harmful.
Vitamin K supplements would antagonize warfarin and increase clotting risk.

stem: A practical nurse is preparing to administer insulin lispro (Humalog) to a client with diabetes. Which time frame is correct for this medication?
answerChoices: Administer 30 minutes before meals.
Administer immediately before or just after meals.
Administer 60 minutes before meals.
Administer at bedtime only.
correctAnswerText: Administer immediately before or just after meals.
rationale: Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting insulin with an onset of 15 minutes. It should be given immediately before or just after meals to match the glucose spike from food intake.
whyWrong: Thirty minutes before meals is too early for rapid-acting insulin and risks hypoglycemia before eating.
Sixty minutes before meals is the timing for regular insulin, not rapid-acting lispro.
Insulin lispro is mealtime insulin, not a basal insulin given only at bedtime.

stem: A nurse is repositioning a client who weighs 200 pounds and cannot assist. Which technique best protects the nurse from back injury?
answerChoices: Bend at the waist and pull the client toward you using upper body strength.
Keep the feet apart, bend the knees, and use leg muscles while keeping the client close.
Lock the knees and twist the torso to slide the client to one side.
Ask one other person to help and lift the client entirely off the bed.
correctAnswerText: Keep the feet apart, bend the knees, and use leg muscles while keeping the client close.
rationale: Proper body mechanics for client repositioning include a wide stance, bent knees, and using leg muscles while keeping the load close to the body. This minimizes spinal strain and prevents back injury.
whyWrong: Bending at the waist places excessive strain on the lumbar spine and risks disc herniation.
Locked knees and twisting combine two high-risk movements that dramatically increase back injury risk.
Lifting a 200-pound client entirely off the bed is unsafe even with two people; sliding and repositioning is preferred.

stem: A practical nurse is caring for a client with a stage 2 pressure injury on the sacrum. Which intervention is most appropriate?
answerChoices: Apply a transparent film dressing to the wound bed.
Cleanse the wound with full-strength hydrogen peroxide.
Reposition the client every 2 hours on a pressure-redistribution surface.
Massage the reddened area around the wound to promote circulation.
correctAnswerText: Reposition the client every 2 hours on a pressure-redistribution surface.
rationale: Repositioning every 2 hours on a pressure-redistribution surface relieves pressure on the sacrum and is the cornerstone of pressure injury management. Without pressure relief, no local wound treatment will be effective.
whyWrong: Transparent film dressings are for shallow wounds or intact skin protection, not a standard treatment for stage 2 pressure injuries.
Hydrogen peroxide is cytotoxic and damages healing tissue; mild wound cleanser or saline is preferred.
Massaging over reddened areas can cause further tissue damage and is contraindicated in pressure injury care.

stem: A nurse is assisting a client with dysphagia during mealtime. Which action reduces the risk of aspiration?
answerChoices: Position the client flat in bed with the head turned to the side.
Have the client tilt the head back to help food move down faster.
Sit the client upright at 90 degrees and offer small bites with adequate time to chew.
Mix all foods with liquid to create a thinner consistency for easier swallowing.
correctAnswerText: Sit the client upright at 90 degrees and offer small bites with adequate time to chew.
rationale: Upright positioning at 90 degrees uses gravity to assist swallowing and reduces aspiration risk. Small bites and adequate chewing time allow the client to manage the bolus safely before swallowing.
whyWrong: Flat positioning increases aspiration risk because gravity does not assist the swallow.
Head tilting back opens the airway and increases aspiration risk.
Thinning foods with liquid can paradoxically increase aspiration risk; thickened liquids are often safer for dysphagia.

stem: During a shift change, a practical nurse receives a report that a client has a new order for a medication the nurse has never administered. What is the nurse's best action?
answerChoices: Look up the medication in a drug reference and administer it as prescribed.
Ask another nurse to administer the medication instead.
Verify the order and consult with the charge nurse or pharmacist before administration.
Hold the medication until the prescriber can be contacted the next day.
correctAnswerText: Verify the order and consult with the charge nurse or pharmacist before administration.
rationale: When a nurse is unfamiliar with a medication, the safe practice is to verify the order and consult available resources (charge nurse, pharmacist) before administration. This protects the client while maintaining timely care.
whyWrong: Simply looking up the medication without consulting may miss critical administration considerations, monitoring requirements, or contraindications.
Delegating to another nurse does not resolve the knowledge gap and may not be within scope.
Holding until the next day delays necessary treatment and is not appropriate.

stem: A practical nurse is caring for four clients on a medical-surgical unit. Which client should the nurse assess first?
answerChoices: A client with pneumonia who asks for assistance to the bathroom and a glass of water.
A client post-abdominal surgery who reports sudden severe pain and abdominal rigidity.
A client with diabetes who asks whether the next blood glucose check can be done now.
A client with hypertension whose scheduled antihypertensive medication is due in 30 minutes.
correctAnswerText: A client post-abdominal surgery who reports sudden severe pain and abdominal rigidity.
rationale: Sudden severe pain with rigidity in a post-abdominal surgery client may indicate a surgical complication such as anastomotic leak, perforation, or peritonitis. This is an emergency requiring immediate assessment.
whyWrong: This is an important comfort/safety need, but it is lower priority than signs of an acute abdominal emergency.
This request is time-sensitive but does not indicate immediate instability compared with sudden abdominal rigidity.
A routine scheduled medication can follow after urgent assessment of a potential postoperative complication.

stem: A practical nurse is caring for a client who refuses to take prescribed medication. What is the nurse's best initial action?
answerChoices: Document the refusal and hold the dose until the next medication round.
Explain the purpose of the medication and explore the client's reason for refusal.
Offer to mix the medication in applesauce to improve tolerance before discussing alternatives.
Notify the healthcare provider immediately to request a different administration route.
correctAnswerText: Explain the purpose of the medication and explore the client's reason for refusal.
rationale: When a client refuses medication, the nurse should first explore the reason for refusal and provide education about the medication's purpose. The client has the right to refuse, and understanding the concern may resolve the issue without escalation.
whyWrong: Documentation is required, but initial nursing care must include assessment and education first.
Changing how a medication is given should follow informed discussion and provider/pharmacy guidance, not precede assessment.
Provider notification may be needed later, but first-line nursing action is to assess concerns and provide teaching.

stem: [RW] During bowel training, a client says, "I can feel the urge and hold it for a short time until I reach the bathroom." Which structure allows this voluntary control?
answerChoices: External anal sphincter
Internal anal sphincter
Rectal stretch receptors
Sigmoid-colon enteric plexus
correctAnswerText: External anal sphincter
rationale: The external anal sphincter is skeletal muscle under voluntary control and allows intentional delay of defecation. The internal anal sphincter is involuntary smooth muscle, and the bowel segments listed do not provide conscious continence control.
whyWrong: The internal anal sphincter functions involuntarily and cannot be consciously controlled.
The ascending colon transports/stores stool but does not provide voluntary continence control.
The duodenum is part of upper GI digestion, not defecation control.

stem: [RW] A practical nurse is preparing a client for provider-ordered eye irrigation after dust exposure. Which item should the nurse gather to hold solution against the eye?
answerChoices: Eye shield
Eye cup
Sterile eyewash bottle
Ocular medication dropper
correctAnswerText: Eye cup
rationale: An eye cup is shaped to fit around the orbit and retain irrigation solution briefly against the eye. The other items are not designed for safe ocular irrigation setup.
whyWrong: An eye shield protects the eye but does not hold irrigation solution against the orbit.
A sterile eyewash bottle can deliver fluid, but the eye cup is the item designed to retain solution against the eye.
An ocular medication dropper delivers drops and is not used as an irrigation reservoir.

stem: [RW] A client with weak hand grip from osteoarthritis keeps dropping a regular toothbrush. Which adaptation should the practical nurse recommend first to support independent oral care?
answerChoices: Use a built-up foam handle on the toothbrush
Switch to a child-size toothbrush with a thinner handle
Hold two toothbrushes together to make brushing faster
Stop brushing and use mouthwash only
correctAnswerText: Use a built-up foam handle on the toothbrush
rationale: A built-up handle increases grasp surface and reduces required finger strength, improving independent use for clients with limited hand function. The other options either worsen grip demands or provide unsafe/inadequate oral care.
whyWrong: A thinner handle usually requires more pinch strength and is harder to control with weak grip.
Combining two toothbrushes is unstable and not a recognized adaptive strategy.
Mouthwash alone does not replace mechanical plaque removal by brushing.

stem: [RW] A client is being taught safe cane use after discharge. Which statements by the client indicate correct understanding? Select all that apply.
answerChoices: I will hold the cane on my stronger side.
I should move my weak leg and cane forward together.
I should set cane height so the top aligns near my wrist crease with my elbow locked straight.
I will wear non-skid shoes when using the cane.
If stairs are difficult, I can ask for help and use handrails.
correctAnswerText: I will hold the cane on my stronger side.
I should move my weak leg and cane forward together.
I will wear non-skid shoes when using the cane.
If stairs are difficult, I can ask for help and use handrails.
rationale: Safe cane technique includes holding the cane on the stronger side, advancing the cane with the weaker leg, using stable footwear, and using help/handrails on stairs as needed. Cane height should be fitted at wrist crease (with slight elbow flexion), not shoulder height.
whyWrong: Locking the elbow reduces control and shock absorption; proper fit keeps a slight elbow flexion.
